raw.notes: Khalkha lineage of Jebtsundamba Khutuktu — Mongolia's spiritual leader equivalent to Dalai Lama

raw.regions_all: Tebriz; Kazvin; İsfahan; Azerbaycan; Horasan; Irak (geçici); Gürcistan; Herat (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Safavids were the dynasty that transformed Iran into a Twelver Shī'ī state, laying the foundations of modern Iranian national identity. Founded through Shāh Ismā'īl's charismatic leadership, the state transformed Isfahan into one of the world's most magnificent capitals under Shāh Abbās. While Ottoman-Safavid rivalry permanently shaped the Sunni-Shī'ī axis of the Middle East, Isfahan's Naqsh-e Jahān Square was built as one of Islamic architecture's most spectacular plazas. The Safavids also institutionalized the Iranian carpet tradition, miniature painting, and Shī'ī theology. The dynasty, which collapsed when the Afghan Ghilzai invasion took Isfahan, permanently determined Iran's political structure.

raw.notes: Mongolian Yuan throne; Northern Yuan extinguished 1635 by Manchus
Genghis Khan's clan; ~16M Y-DNA descendants worldwide; many surviving aristocratic branches

raw.regions_all: Meşhed; İsfahan; Delhi (geçici sefer); Buhara (geçici sefer)
raw.narrative_en: The Afshārids were a short-lived Iranian dynasty founded through Nādir Shāh's extraordinary military genius, entering history with the enormous booty of the Delhi campaign (Koh-i Noor, Peacock Throne). The empire rapidly disintegrated after Nādir Shāh's assassination.

The Musha'sha'ids were an Arab dynasty founded with extreme Shī'ī ideology in the Khuzestan region of southwestern Iran, ruling for 290 years under the protection of marshland geography. They maintained their existence for a long time as Safavid vassals.

The Zands were a short-lived dynasty of Lur origin that established dominance in southern Iran under Karīm Khān's leadership. Using the title 'Wakīl al-Dawla' (regent of the state) instead of 'Padishāh,' Karīm Khān made Shiraz his capital, maintaining a populist and relatively peaceful governance.
The Inju'ids were a small dynasty from the Il-Khanid fiscal tradition that ruled briefly in Fars. They ended by being defeated by the Muzaffarids.

The Muzaffarids rose in Fars and Kirman after the Il-Khanid collapse, as the dynasty of the era when Ḥāfiẓ of Shiraz lived. Under Shāh Shujā's patronage, Shiraz continued to be one of the most brilliant centers of Persian literature. They ended through fratricidal conflicts and Timur's liquidation.

The Pahlavis were Iran's last dynasty, founded through Riżā Shāh's military coup. While Riżā Shāh launched a radical modernization and secularization program, his son Muḥammad Riżā Shāh's White Revolution and the 1953 CIA coup constitute the most controversial pages of Iranian political history. The Pahlavi era, ending with the 1979 Islamic Revolution, is Iran's last monarchic age.

The Qājārs were a dynasty founded by the Qājār tribe of the Oghuz Turks, governing Iran for 145 years. Making Tehran the capital, the Qājārs were prominent for modernization efforts during Nāṣir al-Dīn Shāh's 47-year reign. The Constitutional Revolution (1906) was the most important political transformation of this era. They ended with Riżā Khān's coup.
The Khāns of Astrakhan were a Golden Horde remnant Turkic-Tatar khanate that ruled briefly in the Lower Volga, conquered by Ivan the Terrible.

The Khāns of Qāsimov were a unique political entity that maintained its existence for over two centuries as a Muslim Tatar buffer principality within Russia. Governed by khans from five different lineages, Qāsimov represents one of the rare examples of Islamic governance tradition continuing within the Russian Tsardom.

raw.regions_all: Hokand (Kokand); Fergana; Taşkent; Türkistan şehri; Kırgız toprakları
raw.narrative_en: The Mings established the Khanate of Kokand in the Ferghana Valley, governing one of the last Islamic states to achieve independence in Central Asia. Strengthened by the wealth of cotton production and Silk Road trade, the khanate ended with Russian annexation in 1876.

raw.successor: Sovyet Hârezm Halk Cumhuriyeti (1920) → Türkmenistan/Özbekistan SSC
raw.narrative_en: The Qungrats governed the last independent state of Khwarazm, known as the Khanate of Khiva, as an Uzbek dynasty. While the slave trade became the main pretext for Russian intervention, they were destroyed by the Red Army in 1920.

names.en: Mongol Great Khāns (Ögedey and Toluy descendants, later Yüan Dynasty)

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Mongol Great Khāns (Ögedey and Toluy descendants, later Yüan Dynasty)
raw.regions_all: Moğolistan; Karakurum; Pekin (Hanbalık/Dadu); Çin; Kore; Tibet
raw.narrative_en: The Great Mongol Khāns governed the largest land empire in world history, founded by Genghis Khan. The Mongol conquests that devastated much of Islamic lands created the greatest breaking point in Islamic political history with the destruction of Baghdad (1258). Under Ögedei and Möngke, a vast empire stretching from China to the borders of Europe was administered.

The Khāns of Kazan were a Turkic-Tatar state ruling as the bastion of Islam in the Volga-Ural region, whose conquest by Ivan the Terrible in 1552 created a great shock in the Islamic world. The fall of Kazan symbolizes the beginning of Russia's expansion southward and eastward.

names.en: Khāns of the Golden Horde (Jochi descendants)

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Khāns of the Golden Horde (Jochi descendants)
raw.regions_all: Saray (Volga); Hârezm; Kırım; Batı Sibirya; Kıpçak Bozkırı
raw.successor: Kırım Hanlığı; Kazan Hanlığı; Astrahan Hanlığı; Kāsımov Hanlığı
raw.narrative_en: The Golden Horde was a Mongol-Turkic state ruling vast steppes from southern Russia to Khwarazm as heirs of Genghis Khan's eldest son Jochi. Berke Khan becoming the first Mongol ruler to accept Islam, Islam becoming the official religion under Özbeg Khan, and the alliance with the Mamluks are important turning points in Islamic history. The Mamluk alliance against the Il-Khanids deeply affected the geopolitical balances of the Islamic world. Timur's devastating campaigns fragmented the Golden Horde, preparing the ground for the emergence of the Crimean, Kazan, and Astrakhan khanates.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Giray Khāns of the Crimea
raw.narrative_en: The Giray Khāns of the Crimea were a Turkic-Tatar state established from the Golden Horde's fragmentation, ruling for 350 years as Ottoman vassals and allies. Playing a strategic role in Russo-Ottoman wars, the Crimean Khanate ended with Russian annexation in 1783.

The Chaghatayids ruled in Transoxiana and Eastern Turkestan as heirs of Genghis Khan's second son Chagatai. The western branch Turkified and Islamized, preparing the ground for Timur's rise, while the eastern branch preserved Mongol traditions longer.

raw.successor: Sovyet Buhara Halk Cumhuriyeti (1920) → Özbekistan SSC
raw.narrative_en: The Mangīts, ruling the Bukhara Emirate, governed one of Central Asia's last independent Islamic states as an Uzbek dynasty. Maintaining power with the 'Emīr' title without Genghiside descent, the Mangīts entered Russian protectorate in 1868 and were destroyed by the Red Army in 1920.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Toqay Temūrids or Jānids or Ashtarkhānids
raw.parent_tribe: Tokaytimur b. Cuci kolu (Cengiz soyundan); Astrahan bağlantısı
raw.narrative_en: The Jānids, also known as the Bukhara Khanate, were an Uzbek dynasty that ruled Transoxiana for 150 years after the Shībānids. They collapsed through weakening central authority and Nādir Shāh's pressure.
